note: Reused as the lead image for the Chinese and English shuifang feature to show a compact brewing zone with clear support-tool boundaries near the main vessel, suitable for explaining open waste-water placement and short discard paths.
Reused in the shuifang feature to illustrate local boundary management on tea tables that no longer rely on full-surface drainage.
Reused in the shuifang feature to support discussion of order, support objects, and the visual calm created by bounded wet-action zones.
Reused in the shuifang feature to support discussion of shared tea service and how local waste-water control protects the serving zone from spreading disorder.

Reused as the lead image for the Chinese and English Ming loose-leaf tea history feature to show a reader-facing loose-leaf brewing order with gaiwan, fairness pitcher, and cups; also used to support discussion of how the tea table itself was reorganized after the rise of steeped loose tea.
Reused in the Ming loose-leaf tea history feature as a contrast image for the earlier bowl-surface-centered Song whisked-tea world that loose-leaf steeping gradually displaced.
Reused in the Ming loose-leaf tea history feature to illustrate how loose-leaf brewing entered broad everyday teahouse and urban life beyond elite performance tea culture.
Reused in the Ming loose-leaf tea history feature to illustrate the long afterlife and flexibility of loose-leaf brewing setups in later Chinese tea culture.
Reused as the lead image for the Chinese and English lid-rest feature to show clear brewing-zone boundaries and the kind of tabletop order that makes a dedicated lid-rest discussion meaningful.
Reused in the lid-rest feature to illustrate dry-brewing zoning, tabletop boundaries, and the placement logic of small supporting utensils near the main brewing vessel.
Reused in the lid-rest feature to support discussion of material mood, support-tool boundaries, and how small teaware affects the overall steadiness of a tea table.
Reused as the lead visual for the Chinese and English Famen Temple crypt tea-set history feature to emphasize ordered, coordinated tea utensils rather than generic tea mood imagery.
Reused in the Famen Temple crypt tea-set feature to illustrate functional differentiation within a coordinated tea-utensil system.

Reused in the Famen Temple crypt tea-set feature as a reader-friendly visual analogue for a coordinated tea-set layout, with captions explicitly limiting the comparison and avoiding false claims that it depicts Tang tea itself.
Metal tea strainer resting over a cup; used to illustrate the logic of filtering loose leaves and the relation between vessel and filter.
Close-up of a tea strainer; used for teaware article illustration.
Traditional Sichuan teahouse scene used for the Chinese and English history feature on the return of the teahouse as an urban public space.
Shanghai Huxinting Teahouse image used to illustrate the teahouse as part of urban public and scenic life.
Reused in the Chinese and English tea tasting cup feature as a clean pale-cup visual supporting discussion of liquor color, cup wall traces, and tasting-style observation.
Reused in the tea tasting cup feature to support discussion of comparability, multi-cup observation, and standardized vessel logic.
Reused in the tea tasting cup feature to illustrate pale porcelain surfaces and close-range observation of liquor and aroma traces.
Reused in the tea tasting cup feature to support discussion of recordable comparison, order, and tasting workflow.
Used in the Chinese and English history feature on tea intangible heritage revival to illustrate embodied hand-processing craft at the center of traditional tea heritage.

Used in the tea intangible heritage feature to show that tea heritage begins in tea landscapes, cultivation knowledge, and tea garden management; also reused in the Huangshan Maofeng feature as a mountain-garden visual supporting discussion of Huizhou highland ecology.
Used in the tea intangible heritage feature to illustrate how traditional tea culture is being translated into contemporary retail and youth-facing tea spaces.
Gongfu tea setup with paired tall and short cups, downloaded for the Chinese and English aroma cup feature to illustrate the logic of wenxiangbei and drinking-cup pairing.
Traditional tea service image used in the aroma cup feature to support discussion of the tall-cup and low-cup set as a functional pair rather than pure decoration.

Lead dry-leaf image used in the Chinese and English jasmine tea feature to show scented finished tea as a crafted product rather than a decorative mix.
Used in the jasmine tea feature to show curled scented-leaf structure and variation in tea-base form after scenting.
Used in the jasmine tea feature to illustrate pearl-shaped scented tea and how form changes brewing display and market positioning.
Used in the jasmine tea feature to support discussion of higher-grade scented tea, finer buds, and crafted leaf presentation.

Used in the Chinese and English Xinyang Maojian feature as the lead brewing image to show glass-brewing observation, early-spring green tea liquor clarity, and unfolding leaves relevant to Maojian reading.
Used in the Xinyang Maojian feature to illustrate dry-leaf observation, strand shape, and the reader-facing logic of checking green tea appearance before buying.
Used in the Xinyang Maojian feature to support discussion of liquor brightness, leaf unfolding, and how to read tenderness and cleanliness in a brewed green tea.

Reused in the Xinyang Maojian feature as a process-support visual for fine green tea heat control, with captions explicitly describing it as an illustration of processing logic rather than a literal Xinyang scene.

Used in the spring tea time-consciousness feature as the lead image to show early spring green tea unfolding in real time and to support discussion of pre-Qingming freshness, seasonal timing, and the cultural value of the first cup of spring.

Used in the spring tea time-consciousness feature to illustrate how early spring dry-leaf smallness and tenderness become visible markers in the cultural distinction between pre-Qingming and later spring tea.

Used in the spring tea time-consciousness feature to support discussion of older Chinese tea order, seasonal awareness, and the historical backdrop against which fresh spring tea gained symbolic force.
Used in the spring tea time-consciousness feature to connect first-pick and pre-Qingming arguments back to real tea-mountain timing, picking windows, and agricultural order.
Used in the spring tea time-consciousness feature to show how spring tea helps modern drinkers perceive seasonal arrival through liquor brightness and leaf unfolding.
